class.name: Material
class.brief_description: Virtual base class for applying visual properties to an object, such as color and roughness.
class.description: [Material] is a base resource used for coloring and shading geometry. All materials inherit from it and almost all [VisualInstance3D] derived nodes carry a [Material]. A few flags and parameters are shared between all material types and are configured here. Importantly, you can inherit from [Material] to create your own custom material type in script or in GDExtension.

method.name: _can_do_next_pass
method.description: Only exposed for the purpose of overriding. You cannot call this function directly. Used internally to determine if [member next_pass] should be shown in the editor or not.

method.name: _can_use_depth_bias
method.description: Only exposed for the purpose of overriding. You cannot call this function directly. Used internally to determine if [member depth_bias_constant_factor], [member depth_bias_slope_factor], and [member depth_bias_clamp] should be shown in the editor or not.

method.name: _can_use_render_priority
method.description: Only exposed for the purpose of overriding. You cannot call this function directly. Used internally to determine if [member render_priority] should be shown in the editor or not.

method.name: _get_shader_mode
method.description: Only exposed for the purpose of overriding. You cannot call this function directly. Used internally by various editor tools.

method.name: _get_shader_rid
method.description: Only exposed for the purpose of overriding. You cannot call this function directly. Used internally by various editor tools. Used to access the RID of the [Material]'s [Shader].

method.name: create_placeholder
method.description: Creates a placeholder version of this resource ([PlaceholderMaterial]).

method.name: inspect_native_shader_code
method.description: Only available when running in the editor. Opens a popup that visualizes the generated shader code, including all variants and internal shader code. See also [method Shader.inspect_native_shader_code].

members.member: The maximum (or minimum) depth bias of a fragment. This will clamp the total bias added to each fragment, which helps prevent extreme values that can cause artifacts. It is equivalent to the [code]clamp[/code] parameter of [code]glPolygonOffsetClamp[/code]. A value of [code]0[/code] will disable clamping. The sign of [member depth_bias_clamp] should usually match that of [member depth_bias_constant_factor] and [member depth_bias_slope_factor]. [b]Note:[/b] This only applies to [StandardMaterial3D]s and [ShaderMaterial]s with type "Spatial". [b]Note:[/b] This will only affect triangle primitives, not lines or points. [b]Note:[/b] This is only supported on some platforms. On unsupported platforms, it will have no effect. To detect whether the current platform supports [member depth_bias_clamp], call [method RenderingServer.has_os_feature] with [code]"depth_bias_clamp"[/code]. It is usually supported on the Forward+ and Mobile renderers. D3D12 and Metal always support this feature, while Vulkan support depends on the GPU and driver. It is inconsistently supported on the Compatibility renderer, but generally works on desktop platforms.

The scalar factor controlling the constant depth value added to each fragment. This is commonly used to prevent z-fighting by forcing one mesh to appear on top of the other. It is equivalent to the [code]units[/code] parameter of [code]glPolygonOffset[/code]. This will add the same small amount of bias to all fragments, which is useful for meshes that are mostly parallel to the camera. Because Godot uses a "reversed-z" depth buffer, negative values will push fragments away from the camera, while positive values will pull them closer. It is recommended to use a value of [code]-1[/code] or [code]1[/code] in most cases, and to set [member depth_bias_slope_factor] to the same value. [b]Note:[/b] This only applies to [StandardMaterial3D]s and [ShaderMaterial]s with type "Spatial". [b]Note:[/b] This will only affect triangle primitives, not lines or points. To make lines or points appear on top of a triangle mesh, apply a negative depth bias to the triangle mesh, which will push it behind them.

The scalar factor applied to a fragment’s slope in depth bias calculations. This is commonly used to prevent z-fighting by forcing one mesh to appear on top of the other. It is equivalent to the [code]factor[/code] parameter of [code]glPolygonOffset[/code]. This will add more bias to fragments that are at a steep angle relative to the camera. Because Godot uses a "reversed-z" depth buffer, negative values will push fragments away from the camera, while positive values will pull them closer. It is recommended to use a value of [code]-1[/code] or [code]1[/code] in most cases, and to set [member depth_bias_constant_factor] to the same value. [b]Note:[/b] This only applies to [StandardMaterial3D]s and [ShaderMaterial]s with type "Spatial". [b]Note:[/b] This will only affect triangle primitives, not lines or points. To make lines or points appear on top of a triangle mesh, apply a negative depth bias to the triangle mesh, which will push it behind them.

Sets the [Material] to be used for the next pass. This renders the object again using a different material. [b]Note:[/b] [member next_pass] materials are not necessarily drawn immediately after the source [Material]. Draw order is determined by material properties, [member render_priority], and distance to camera. [b]Note:[/b] This only applies to [StandardMaterial3D]s and [ShaderMaterial]s with type "Spatial".

Sets the render priority for objects in 3D scenes. Higher priority objects will be sorted in front of lower priority objects. In other words, all objects with [member render_priority] [code]1[/code] will render on top of all objects with [member render_priority] [code]0[/code]. [b]Note:[/b] This only applies to [StandardMaterial3D]s and [ShaderMaterial]s with type "Spatial". [b]Note:[/b] This will not impact how transparent objects are sorted relative to opaque objects or how dynamic meshes will be sorted relative to other opaque meshes. This is because all transparent objects are drawn after all opaque objects and all dynamic opaque meshes are drawn before other opaque meshes.

constants.constant: Maximum value for the [member render_priority] parameter.
Minimum value for the [member render_priority] parameter.
